revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Higashida Ōtsuka Kofun]][[ja:東田大塚古墳]]






 


{{Short description|Ancient Japanese tomb}}
{{Draft topics|east-asia}}
{{AfC topic|geo}}

{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|qid=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}}|
image=File:Higaida_ohtsuka_kohun_aerial.jpg|coordinates={{Coord|34.543528|N|135.833389|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|location={{ill|Sakurai|qid=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}|architectural_styles={{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11608504|1=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun}}|part_of={{ill|Makimuku Kofun Cluster|qid=Q11608501|1=Makimuku Kofun Cluster}}}}
[[Higaida Ōtsuka Kofun]] (東田大塚古墳) is a [[Kofun]] found on the premises of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=春成 |first1=秀爾 |last2=小林 |first2=謙一 |last3=坂本 |first3=稔 |last4=今村 |first4=峯雄 |last5=尾嵜 |first5=大真 |last6=藤尾 |first6=慎一郎 |last7=西本 |first7=豊弘 |last8=Harunari |first8=Hideji |last9=Kobayashi |first9=Kenichi |last10=Sakamoto |first10=Minoru |last11=Imamura |first11=Mineo |last12=Ozaki |first12=Hiromasa |last13=Fujio |first13=Shinichiro |last14=Nishimoto |first14=Toyohiro |date=March 20dd |title=古墳出現期の炭素14th year 代測定 |url=https://rekihaku.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/1889/export/bibtex |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告, Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History |volume=163 |pages=133–176}}</ref>. It is near {{ill|Makimuku_Station|en|Makimuku_Station|qid=Q4980794|lt=Makimuku Station|1=Makimuku Station}}.<ref>https://www.city.sakurai.lg.jp/section/yamato-travel/assets/pdf/09-MAP.pdf</ref>It is a Kofun of [[Makimuku ruins]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}

It is 96m long and points west<ref>{{Cite book |last=Kidder |first=J. Edward |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aY4BEAAAQBAJ&dq=Higaida&pg=PA249 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai: Archaeology, History, and Mythology |date=2007-02-28 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8 |pages=249 |language=en}}</ref>

Higaida Otsuka Kofun is located in Makimuku ruins and from the Kofun period which spans from the 3rd to the 6th century. Radiocarbon dating by was done on the site and estimated it was made from AD 240 to 260.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=秀爾 |first1=春成 |last2=謙一 |first2=小林 |last3=稔 |first3=坂本 |last4=峯雄 |first4=今村 |last5=大真 |first5=尾嵜 |last6=慎一郎 |first6=藤尾 |last7=豊弘 |first7=西本 |date=20dd-03-31 |title=古墳出現期の炭素14th year 代測定 |url=https://cir.nii.ac.jp/crid/1390572174043065472 |journal=国立歴史民俗博物館研究報告 = Bulletin of the National Museum of Japanese History |language=ja |volume=163 |pages=133–176 |doi=10.15024/00001872}}</ref>

The findings specifically for the Higaida Otsuka Kofun suggest that its construction and initial usage period likely fall between AD 240 to 260. This helped provide insight into the early kofun period.<ref name=":0" />

Higaida-otsuka is a mound located south of {{ill|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|en|Makimuku Yazuka Kofun|qid=Q11608503|lt=Yazuka|1=Makimuku Yatsuka Kofun}}. It's also known as the East Field Large Mound, but it's not very big. In 1998, a 20-meter-wide moat was found nearby. Archaeologists found paving stones, haniwa (clay figures), and parts of a clay coffin. They also discovered pots from the western Inland Sea and Tòkai areas. One notable Tòkai pot, with fine quality and painted decoration, is called "palace style." Additionally, a small wooden boat was found along with Makimuku 3 New-type pottery as part of the grave goods..<ref name=":1" />{{rp|251}}
